slides.title: Mahalanobis Distance for Robust Out-of-Distribution Detection
slides.notes: Introduces the core method: Mahalanobis distance-based confidence scoring for detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in neural networks.

steps.heading: Problem Statement
steps.content: Neural networks often misclassify out-of-distribution (OOD) samples as in-distribution, leading to unreliable predictions.

steps.heading: Proposed Solution
steps.content: Introduces a Mahalanobis distance-based confidence score to quantify how far a sample is from the learned distribution.

steps.heading: Key Insight
steps.content: Mahalanobis distance captures the covariance structure of the data, making it more robust than Euclidean distance for detecting OOD samples.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: This method improves detection accuracy and robustness, especially under challenging conditions like limited data or adversarial attacks.

slides.title: Feature Visualization and Performance Comparison
slides.notes: Figure 1 shows feature visualization, classification accuracy, and ROC curves for ResNet. The Mahalanobis method outperforms Softmax and Euclidean distance.

steps.heading: Feature Visualization (a)
steps.content: t-SNE visualization shows clear separation of classes, indicating the model learns distinct feature representations.

steps.heading: Classification Accuracy (b)
steps.content: Mahalanobis achieves higher accuracy on CIFAR-100 and SVHN, demonstrating its effectiveness in handling complex data.

steps.heading: ROC Curves (c)
steps.content: Mahalanobis has the highest TPR at low FPR, indicating superior OOD detection capability compared to Softmax and Euclidean.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: The method is robust and effective across different datasets, providing a strong baseline for OOD detection.

slides.title: Algorithm for Mahalanobis Distance Computation
slides.notes: Algorithm 1 details the computation of the Mahalanobis distance-based confidence score for a test sample.

steps.heading: Input
steps.content: The algorithm takes a test sample, logistic regression weights, noise, and Gaussian distribution parameters.

steps.heading: Process
steps.content: For each layer, it finds the closest class, adds noise to the sample, and computes a confidence score based on the Mahalanobis distance.

steps.heading: Output
steps.content: Returns a confidence score for the test sample, which is the weighted sum of scores from all layers.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: This algorithm provides a principled way to quantify uncertainty and detect OOD samples using the model's internal representations.

slides.title: Class-Incremental Learning Algorithm
slides.notes: Algorithm 2 shows how to update the Mahalanobis distance classifier for class-incremental learning, allowing the model to learn new classes without forgetting old ones.

steps.heading: Input
steps.content: The algorithm takes a new class's samples, along with the current mean and covariance of all classes.

steps.heading: Update Process
steps.content: Computes the new class mean and covariance, then updates the shared covariance matrix to include the new class.

steps.heading: Output
steps.content: Returns the updated mean and covariance for all classes, enabling the model to adapt to new classes.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: This algorithm enables the model to learn new classes incrementally while maintaining performance on previously learned classes.

slides.title: Performance Comparison and Validation
slides.notes: Table 1 and Table 2 compare the proposed method with baselines and show its superior performance under various conditions.

steps.heading: Table 1: Baseline Comparison
steps.content: The Mahalanobis method outperforms ODIN and baseline methods in TNR, AUROC, and detection accuracy for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution data.

steps.heading: Table 2: Validation Scenarios
steps.content: The method performs well under extreme conditions, including small training data and random labels, showing robustness.

steps.heading: Key Metrics
steps.content: High TNR (True Negative Rate) and AUROC scores indicate the method is effective at distinguishing OOD samples.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: The method is not only accurate but also robust, making it suitable for real-world applications with noisy or limited data.

slides.title: Performance Under Extreme Conditions
slides.notes: Figure 3 shows the performance of the method under extreme scenarios: small training data and random labels.

steps.heading: Small Training Data (a)
steps.content: The method maintains high AUROC even with very limited training data, outperforming baselines.

steps.heading: Random Labels (b)
steps.content: The method is robust to random labels, maintaining high performance compared to baselines.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: This demonstrates the method's resilience to data scarcity and label noise, making it practical for real-world deployment.

steps.heading: Conclusion
steps.content: The method is highly effective and robust, providing reliable OOD detection under challenging conditions.

slides.title: Comparison with Other Methods
slides.notes: Table 3 compares the method with other approaches for detecting known and unknown attacks.

steps.heading: Attack Detection
steps.content: The method achieves high AUROC scores for both known and unknown attacks, outperforming other methods.

steps.heading: Feature Ensemble
steps.content: Using feature ensemble and input pre-processing further improves performance, especially for unknown attacks.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: The method is effective at detecting adversarial attacks, making it suitable for security-critical applications.

steps.heading: Conclusion
steps.content: The method provides a comprehensive solution for OOD detection, including robustness to adversarial attacks.

slides.title: Class-Incremental Learning Results
slides.notes: Figure 4 shows the performance of the method in class-incremental learning on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet datasets.

steps.heading: AUC vs. Number of Classes (a)
steps.content: The method maintains high AUC as new classes are added, outperforming Softmax and Euclidean distance.

steps.heading: Base Class Accuracy (b)
steps.content: The method preserves high base class accuracy even as new classes are added, indicating good generalization.

steps.heading: Impact
steps.content: The method is effective in class-incremental learning, maintaining performance on old classes while learning new ones.

steps.heading: Conclusion
steps.content: The method is suitable for lifelong learning scenarios, where models need to learn new classes without forgetting old ones.

slides.title: Summary and Conclusion
slides.notes: The Mahalanobis distance-based method is a robust and effective solution for OOD detection and class-incremental learning.

steps.heading: Key Contributions
steps.content: Proposed a Mahalanobis distance-based confidence score for OOD detection and a method for class-incremental learning.

steps.heading: Performance
steps.content: Outperforms existing methods in accuracy, robustness, and generalization under various conditions.

steps.heading: Applications
steps.content: Suitable for real-world applications requiring reliable OOD detection, such as security and autonomous systems.

steps.heading: Future Work
steps.content: Further research can explore integrating the method with other techniques for even better performance.

slides.notes: Thank you for your attention. Questions?

steps.heading: Thank You
steps.content: Thank you for your attention. We appreciate your time and interest in our work.
